coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity have been widely used in the construction of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors due to their advantages of cost-effectiveness, high stability, good biocompatibility, and ease of modification. Furthermore, the colorimetric and fluorescence sensors, which are effective approaches for detecting bioactive small-molecule compounds, have been extensively explored due to their simple operation and high sensitivity. Recent significant researches have focused on designing various sensors based on nanozymes with peroxidase- and oxidase-like activity for the colorimetric and fluorescence analysis of different analytes. In this review, recent developments (from 2018 to present) in the colorimetric and fluorescent analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials were summarized. In addition, the challenges and design strategies in developing colorimetric and fluorescent assays with high performance and specific sensing were discussed.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in the colorimetric and fluorescence analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity have been widely used in the construction of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors due to their advantages of cost-effectiveness, high stability, good biocompatibility, and ease of modification. Furthermore, the colorimetric and fluorescence sensors, which are effective approaches for detecting bioactive small-molecule compounds, have been extensively explored due to their simple operation and high sensitivity. Recent significant researches have focused on designing various sensors based on nanozymes with peroxidase- and oxidase-like activity for the colorimetric and fluorescence analysis of different analytes. In this review, recent developments (from 2018 to present) in the colorimetric and fluorescent analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials were summarized. In addition, the challenges and design strategies in developing colorimetric and fluorescent assays with high performance and specific sensing were discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioactive small-molecule compounds have small molecular weights that can pass through the cell membrane, interact with biomolecules to alter cell functions or produce specific biological effects. They have important application value in drug development, chemical biology, and chemical ecology, etc. [1]. The bioactive small-molecule compounds usually refer to the compounds that have been clinically tested and approved for the treatment of diseases, such as sulfamethazine (SD), sulfadimethoxine (SDM), sulfathiazole (ST), and captopril [2], natural small-molecule compounds extracted or synthesized from natural products, such as rosmarinus acid and hypoxanthine [3], and chemical compounds used to study the functions and interactions of biomolecules, such as biothiols, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, choline, dopamine (DA), and amino acids [4]. The detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds can provide valuable references in discovering and optimizing compounds with potential drug activity, promoting new drug development, and revealing disease mechanisms [5], etc. Traditional techniques have been widely used for the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds. For example, fluorescence spectroscopy [6], chromatography [7], the combination of chromatography and mass spectrometry (liquid chromatography- and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry) [8], electrochemistry [9], chemiluminescence [10], and colorimetric method [11], have been used in the analysis of DA. The methods of chromatography and mass spectrometry can detect the analytes with very low concentrations, but these methods require expensive instruments, necessitate complex operations, and time-consuming.

The spectral technologies with the advantages of rapid and efficient for sample detection, have become an effective mean for environmental monitoring and biological analysis [12]. For example, Yu et al. [13] reported a strategy to incorporate multiple reaction sites on a chiral 1,1′-bi-2,2′-naphthol (BINOL)-based fluorescent probe, which can respond specifically to D-arginine among the amino acids. A good linear relationship was obtained with arginine concentration in the range of 0–5 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) value of 50 nM. Jabariyan et al. [14] have successfully synthesized a lanthanum-containing mesoporous MCM-41 (La-MCM-41) with high inherent peroxidase (POD)-like activity and was applied in the colorimetric detection of glucose, which has a linear dynamic range from 50 to 1000 μM and a LOD of 37.5 μM in aqueous solution. In spectral detection applications, specific bindings between enzymes and substrates are often used to improve the selectivity of methods. As compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes have obvious advantages, such as facile synthesis, tunable catalytic activity, suitability to severe environments, ease of modification, excellent stability, and low cost, which are widely used in the fields of biosensing, chemical analysis, and therapeutics of some diseases [15]. Currently, many nanomaterials that have natural enzyme-like characteristics, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), POD, oxidase (OXD), catalase (CAT), and hydrolase, have been reported [16]. Among them, POD and OXD nanozymes are the most widely investigated, which can efficiently catalyze the substrates, such as 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,2′-azino-bis-3-ethyl-benzothiazoline-6- sulfonic acid (ABTS), and o-phenylene-diamine dihydrochloride (OPD) to generate chromogenic products, accompanying with the change of fluorescence and colorimetric signals with or without of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [17].

This review aims to highlight the recent advancements and applications of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors based on nanomaterials with enzyme-like (especially POD- and OXD-like) activity for the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds. Firstly, the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials was introduced in brief, including the classification of nanozymes and the corresponding material types. Then, the recent applications (2018 to present) of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for detecting bioactive small-molecule compounds were summarized, mainly including DA, arginine, glucose, H2O2, xanthine, D-penicillamine, procyanidin (OPC), catechin, microcystin-LR, AA, catechol, choline, cholesterol, captopril, sarcosine, biothiols (glutathione, GSH; L-cysteine, Cys; homocysteine, Hcy), rosmarinus acid, hypoxanthine, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), fluoride, SD, SDM, ST, and casein phosphopeptides (CPP). Finally, the challenges and future developments in the nanomaterials-based colorimetric and fluorescence sensors were discussed. This review may be helpful for readers to understand the construction principle and mechanisms, as well as the design of novel colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for highly effective, low-cost, quick, and sensitive detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview of enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Functional nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity (nanozymes), which can catalyze enzyme-substrate reactions [18], have long self-life, cost-effectiveness, high surface area, and controllable in size and adjustable in function [19]. Great efforts have been devoted to developing various nanozymes, such as transition metal oxides [20], novel metals [21], and carbon-based materials [22]. Nanozymes can be classified into three main categories based on their applications in analytical chemistry. The hydrolases mimics [23] mainly include carbon-based materials, such as carbon dots, graphene, graphene quantum dots, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanospheres [24], monolayer functionalized AuNP, and MOF-based (Zn, Zr, Ce, and Cu) materials. It can catalyze the hydrolysis of chemical bonds, such as the nucleotides and phosphate groups [25]. The oxidoreductases mimics, which can be further classified into SOD [26], OXD [27], CAT [28], and POD [29], usually are Mn, Fe, Au, Ag, Cu, Pt, and Ce-based nanomaterials. In addition, the transferases are an important type of enzymes, including aminopherase, glycosyl transferase, trans methylase, and phosphorylase [30].

There are several factors that will affect the activity of nanozymes, such as the size and morphology, surface modification, and doping of other elements on nanomaterials, as well as the reaction conditions. 1) The catalytic efficiency of nanozymes depends on the size of nanomaterials, which determines the active sites because of the difference in specific surface area. Generally, the nanomaterials with a smaller size have a higher surface-to-volume ratio that can expose more active sites, leading to a better catalytic activity [31]. 2) The morphology, such as the Pd octahedra has better performance than Pd nanocages [32], and Fe3O4 octahedra performed better than its spherical shape, because the octahedra has a lower surface energy [33]. 3) For the reaction conditions, the activity of nanozymes are usually influenced by the pH and temperature. For example, an acidic condition can promote the POD-mimicking activity, but neutral and alkaline conditions are favorable for CAT-like property of nanozymes [34]. 4) The surface modification, the modifications on the surface of nanozymes will affect the exposure of the active sites and may reduce the reaction efficiency [35]; 5) The doping of other elements is an efficient way to improve the catalytic activity of nanozymes. For instance, silicon doped CoO nanorods has a better POD-like property than pure CoO, due to the increase in oxygen vacancy [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric sensors for the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds are mainly based on nanomaterials (with POD-, OXD-, and CAT-like activities, etc.) catalyzing the substrate to generate product having an obvious absorption peak at UV-Vis spectra. When adding bioactive small-molecule compounds, the absorbance will be changed. As shown in Table 1, the nanozymes with POD- and OXD-like activities were the most frequently used in the colorimetric detections of bioactive small-molecule compounds, including H2O2, AA, GSH, DA, glucose, D-penicillamine, xanthine, and catechin, etc.

OXD can catalyze the substrates into oxidized products with the help of molecular oxygen or different oxidizing reagents like H2O2. For instance, Co3O4 and CeO2 nanoparticles possess OXD-like activity that can catalytically oxidize TMB without the H2O2 [89]. AA, which protects the body from the harmful effects of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, plays a significant role in preventing cancer, scurvy, and cardiovascular diseases. Yang et al. [65] developed a colorimetric sensor for the detection of AA based on the high OXD-like activity of CeO2-Co3O4 nanocomposite (CeO2-Co3O4 NC), which can provide enough active sites and accelerate electron transfer in TMB, leading to the formation of blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB). The oxTMB will be reduced when AA was added, resulting in the decrease of color and absorbance of solution. A good linear relationship was obtained with AA concentration in the range of 1.0 − 500 μM and the LOD value is 0.25 μM.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), as a type of porous crystalline materials, are assembled through divalent metal cations and imidazolate, which have excellent light harvesting and electron transportation properties. ATP is a terribly important biomolecule, which is the direct energy source of cellular activity and plays a vital role in various biological processes of organisms such as biosynthesis, DNA replication, and cell signaling. The detection of ATP concentration is beneficial for the prevention or treatment of some diseases, such as cardiovascular disease, Parkinson’s disease, cancer, and anemia [90]. Yin et al. [66] prepared different ZIFs nanomaterials of highly light-responsive OXD-like activity, including ZIF-1, ZIF-7, ZIF-8, ZIF-67, and ZIF-90, which can catalyze TMB to produce blue oxTMB under light irradiation. ATP can specifically compete for Zn2+ binding sites with 2-formalimidazole, resulting in the destruction of ZIF-90 structure and a decrease in the absorbance at 652 nm ( Fig. 1). A good linear relationship was obtained with the ATP concentration in the range of 10 − 240 μM and the LOD is 3.85 μM. In addition, on the basis that phenolic pollutants can impact the reaction kinetics diversely on different ZIFs, a sensor array was constructed and successfully applied to differentiate five phenolic pollutants in lake water samples.

The graphene (GO)-based materials can promote electron transfer between substrates and nanozymes [91]. Furthermore, V6O13 has many active sites due to the flexible structure of the coordination polyhedron and the alternating single-bilayer structure, which can enable the target objects (such as O2 and other molecules) to be inserted and extracted reversibly. Song et al. [67] prepared a V6O13-rGO NCs with OXD-like catalytic activity through a hydrothermal method. The activity of V6O13 was increased through the modification of rGO, and the chemical reaction rate of the nanozyme was enhanced by fluoride capping. Therefore, fluoride can be detected through the enzymatic response of V6O13-rGO NCs towards TMB. A good linearity was obtained with the fluoride concentration in the range of 0.02–3.5 mM, and the LOD is 0.015 mM.

Pd nanocrystals show excellent OXD and POD-like catalytic activities. The combination of MIL-125-NH2 and Pd nanocrystals may result in a synergy effect, which can greatly improve the OXD-like activity and avoid the poor molecular recognition and controllability of undecorated Pd nanocrystals. On the other hand, CPP is a bioactive peptide containing phosphoserine residues, which can significantly promote the calcium absorption and show a positive effect on the calcium metabolism [92]. Moreover, CCP also has anti-tumor and immunoregulatory functions. As shown in Fig. 2, Yin et al. [68] synthesized a polyhedral MIL-125-NH2 @Pd nanoparticle with light-responsive OXD-like activity through a simple solvothermal method for the determination of CPP. The developed method has a linear range of 10–100 μg/mL with LOD as low as 2.9 μg/mL. To regulate the catalytic activity of nanomaterials, the biomolecules or other polymers can be used. For example, Li et al. [69] prepared a covalent organic framework (Tph-BT COF) with excellent photocatalytic activity by Schiff base reaction. The single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) was used to regulate its OXD and POD mimetic activity. Under the illumination of diode light, Tph-BT exhibited outstanding OXD activity, which can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce blue oxTMB. ssDNA, especially those with poly-thymidine sequences, can significantly inhibit its OXD activity, which lays a foundation for the construction of colorimetric sensors.

Some reports found that the bimetallic nanomaterials have enhanced catalytic performance and displayed ability to catalyze multistep reactions. Maitri et al. [70] used a multifunctional nanozyme Cu-Pt nanoalloys for the colorimetric detection of AA and identification of mammalian cells. It shows excellent performance for the sensitive detection of AA in the sub-micromolar concentration range and has reasonably biocompatible to the human liver cells and are actively internalized in the cell cytoplasm. Peng et al. [71] prepared a Co-Fe Prussian blue analogue nanocube in situ with OXD-like activity for the colorimetric detection of AA, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), α-glucosidase (α-Glu), and ascorbic acid oxidase (AAO). The absorbance of product decreases with the increase in AA concentration ( Fig. 3). A linear fitting relationship between the absorbance and AA concentration is in the range of 1.0 − 15 μM with the LOD value of 0.399 μM. In addition, the linear ranges for ALP, α-Glu, and AAO are 5–120, 10–200, and 0.25–5.0 U/L with LODs of 0.91, 8.54, and 0.16 U/L, respectively.

In the case of colorimetric analysis, the sensing mechanism is usually based on a color change caused by the chromogenic substrates and nanozyme (POD-like activity)-mediated catalysis reaction [77]. In the presence of H2O2, nanomaterials with POD-like activity can react with TMB to generate oxTMB that has the maximum absorption peak at 652 nm, accompanying a change in the solution color from colorless to blue. The free radicals generated by the action of nanozymes on H2O2 cause the oxidation of TMB, including hydroxyl radical (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide anion radical (·O2 -). It is considered that ·OH is an important and common intermediate in the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 [93]. Wang et al. [72] prepared a two water-stable Fe (Ⅲ)-based MOFs, which exhibits inherent POD-like activity that can catalyze the oxidation of TMB and OPD in the presence of H2O2. When adding the antioxidants AA to the system, the absorbance and color of solution will be decreased. The linear relationship was obtained with the AA concentration in the range of 37.91 − 341.23 μM, and the LOD is 15.92 μM, which is comparable or even better than the other methods. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4, Peng et al. [73] prepared the CoCO3 using Co2+ and carbonate in situ at room temperature, which has the inherent POD-like activity and can be used for the detection of AA, H2O2, and glucose in the ranges of 1.0–25.0, 5.0–75.0, and 12.5–1000.0 μM with the LOD values of 0.79, 1.39, and 10.3 μM, respectively.

The activity of nanozymes is crucial in the detection of biological small-molecule compounds, and the different modification methods were commonly used to enhance their enzyme-like activity. Phytic acid (PA) has six phosphate groups, which can chelate with Cu2+ to form similar organic-inorganic complexes. As shown in Fig. 5, Zhang et al. [74] have synthesized the PA/Cu3(PO4)2⋅3 H2O through a facile co-precipitation method. The catalytic activity of PA/Cu3(PO4)2⋅3 H2O was greatly increased by PA modification. The linearity for the detection of H2O2 is in the range of 0.1–5.0 mM and LOD is 0.079 mM.

To further improve the detection sensitivity, the synergistic effect between materials can be utilized. For instance, Shi et al. [75] have successfully prepared a gold nanoparticles@MoS2 nanosheets (AuNPs@MoS2 NSs) with high POD-like activity for the colorimetric detection of biothiols. The synergistic impact between AuNPs and MoS2 NSs accelerates the reaction of TMB with the presence of H2O2. The biothiols can restrain this process, leading to the decrease in absorbance. Through this sensing mechanism, the detection of biothiols based on the high POD-like activity of AuNPs@MoS2 NSs can be achieved. The signal responses to Hcy, GSH, and Cys are linear with their concentrations in the range of 1.0–100 μM, and the LOD values are 0.93, 0.71, and 0.73 μM, respectively. This method is suitable for the detection of the total amount of these biological thiols in actual samples. In reality, biothiols, including GSH, Cys, and Hcy, which play a crucial role in several physiological processes, are significant importance in maintaining the oxidation-reduction physiological state. An abnormal level of biothiols is closely related to various diseases, like vas disease, nonheritable immunological disorder syndrome, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer [94].

To improve the detection selectivity, cascade reactions between natural enzymes and nanozymes have been used. The abnormal glucose level caused by polygenic disorder will lead to severe complications, including coma, seizures, and organ harm. The accurate detection of glucose level is the first step for the effective treatment of diabetes mellitus. Li et al. [76] prepared a ruthenium oxide/reduced graphene oxide (RuO2/rGO) heterostructure with high POD-like activity through one-pot hydrothermal method using graphene oxides and RuCl3. Then, the glucose was detected by the cascade reaction of glucose oxidase (GOx) and POD-like activity of RuO2/rGO. The established colorimetric method has good sensitivity and selectivity for glucose detection with a LOD of 3.34 μM and a linear range of 0 − 1500 μM. In addition, colorimetric sensors based on the POD-like activity of nanomaterials can also be used in the detection of dopamine, H2O2, choline, and uric acid, etc.

CAT is a natural typical bioenzyme found in living organisms, which can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into molecular oxygen and H2O. Many nanomaterials, such as metals and metal oxides exhibit CAT-like activity. When constructing colorimetric sensors to detect bioactive small-molecules, they are generally achieved by combining with other enzyme cascade reactions, which is an effective signal amplification method. Yang et al. [77] prepared a Fe3O4 @C@MnO2 composite with POD, CAT, and OXD-like activities, which can be used for the detection of DA based on its OXD-like activity. In addition, it is also possible to synthesize nanozymes with porous structure, not only producing rich active site, but also showing larger surface area, and conducive to improving the catalytic activity. Wu et al. [95] synthetized Fe-N-C single-atom nanozymes with atomically dispersed FeN4 active sites anchored on a three-dimensional hierarchically ordered microporous-mesoporous-macroporous nitrogen doped carbon matrix (3DOM Fe-N-C). The three-dimensional hierarchically ordered porous structure may accelerate mass transfer and improve the accessibility of active sites, which effectively enhance the tri-enzyme-mimic activities of Fe-N-C single-atom nanozymes, comprising OXD-, POD-, and CAT-like activities. Based on its excellent POD-like activity, 3DOM Fe-N-C can degrade rhodamine B by·OH generated in the presence of H2O2, and its degradation rate reached 94% after 4 h. Recently, the research is also focused on the magnetic metal oxides due to their excellent superparamagnetic property. However, bare magnetic metal oxides are not satisfactory in terms of catalytic efficiency. To balance superparamagnetic and high catalytic activity, the hybridization of magnetic metal oxides with other compounds has been proven to be an excellent method. Liu et al. [78] reported the synthesis of a novel multi-metal hybrid Fe3O4-Ag-MnO2 nanocomposites, which possess three types of enzyme-mimicking activity, including POD-, CAT-, and OXD-like activities. The superparamagnetic property of Fe3O4 improves the analysis and detection efficiency, and the Ag-MnO2 exhibits high catalytic activity, which can achieve the fast, sensitive, and intuitive multicolor detection of Staphylococcus aureus.

The excess accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells will cause oxidative damage to living systems, such as cell function degeneration, cell death, and various diseases [96]. Selectively regulating the intracellular ROS level by employing nanozymes with SOD-like property through the dismutation reaction, which converts·O2 - to generate H2O and O2, can alleviate the ROS associated injury and inflammation. The metal nanoclusters, including Au, Ag, Pt, Pd, and Cu-based, are widely applied in biological analysis for their excellent properties, such as high photochemical stability, enzyme-like activity, and large Stokes shift. Yang et al. [79] have prepared a PtCu nanocages (PtCu NCs) with hollow interior and rough exterior, which can act as an efficient ROS scavenger to eliminate the toxic ROS, including ·OH, 1O2, and ·O2 -, alleviating oxidative stress based on its POD, CAT, and SOD-like activity. Based on the POD mimic activity, the established colorimetric method was successfully applied in the detection of Cys, Hcy, and GSH with linear range of 0.5–40 μM. The LODs values of Cys, Hcy, and GSH are 0.28, 1.48, and 0.75 μM, respectively. Metal NCs are composed of several to hundreds of atoms, and their growth rate is controlled by the coordination ligands. The selection of ligands can affect the formation of nanoparticles [97]. Researchers have paid their attention to proteins, thiols, and other substances to regulate the generation speed and size of nanoclusters. Liu et al. [80] prepared a uniform and monodisperse metal Cu NCs using cysteamine as a template and hydrazine hydrate as a reductant, which shows excellent POD-, AAO-, CAT-, and SOD-like activities. Based on the POD-like activity of Cu NCs, the established method was applied in the detection of GSH with the LOD value of 0.89 μM. In addition, MOF is a new class of porous material linked by metal nodes and organic ligands. Its striking feature is highly programmable topology, like the encoded scaffold of enzyme. The SOD-like activity of bimetallic MOFs can outperform 3–8 times higher than that of monometallic metals mimics. Wu et al. [98] utilized the bimetal metal-organic framework (MOF-818) platform for mimicking of SOD, which is an important antioxidant enzyme that specifically scavenges superoxide anions. The high SOD-like activity of MOF-818, which results from the bimetal synergistic catalysis, has certain potential in colorimetric detection of biological small-molecule compounds.

Hydrolase can catalyze the hydrolysis of chemical bonds such as nucleotides and phosphate groups. The repeating units of ZIF-8 has a similar geometric structure to that of the active center of human carbonic anhydrase II (hCAII). ZIF-8 exhibits hCAII, promiscuous esterase, and acetylcholinesterase (AChE)-like activities. The prolonged or irregular use of sulfonamides (SAAs) in farm animals for the treatment of various bacterial infections can lead to their residues in milk and dairy products. The SAAs residues can cause human health problems, such as resistance to antibiotics, allergic reactions, and toxic effects. In our previous report [81], a colorimetric method based on ZIF-8 was developed for the detection of SAAs in cow milk. The hCAII-like activity of ZIF-8 can hydrolyze p-nitrophenyl acetate (pNPA) to p-nitrophenol (pNP), showing the maximum absorption peak at 405 nm. The possible chemical action mechanism is that the Zn2+ functions as a nucleophilic Lewis acid, lowering the pKa of a bound water molecule, so forming a reactive hydroxyl ion to catalyze the chemical reaction of esters ( Fig. 6). The established method was used for the detection of SD, SDM, and ST with the LOD values of 4.3, 3.2, and 3.9 μМ, respectively. Fu et al. [99] have synthetized a porous ZIF-90 nanozyme via Zn2+ and imidazole, in which Zn2+ is the active center and imidazole is the ligand, mimicking the active sites of organophosphate hydrolase (OPH). In addition, CeO2 has been demonstrated to be a promising material to mimic phosphatase through the interaction between surface Ce and the phosphoryl oxygen (P = O) of substrates for ester hydrolysis. The electron density of surface Ce among CeO2 shapes is facet dependent in the order of (100)> (110)> (111). Ce atoms on the (111) surface with low electron density can effectively promote the adsorption and hydrolysis of substrate at room temperature [100].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent sensors are frequently used to detect biomolecules, due to their high sensitivity, high specificity, resistance to light scattering, and ease of use [101]. The mechanisms of fluorescence sensing systems are mainly based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer, fluorescence quenching, electron transfer, energy transfer, aggregation-induced emission enhancement, aggregation-induced emission quenching, aggregation-induced red-shift emission, and static quenching [102]. The fluorescence sensors for bioactive small-molecule compounds detection are mainly based on nanomaterials (with POD-, OXD-, and others-like activities) catalyzing the substrate to generate product having fluorescence. When adding bioactive small-molecule compounds, the fluorescence intensities of products will be changed. As shown in Table 1, the nanozymes are mainly POD- and OXD-like activity used for the fluorescence detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds, including H2O2, GSH, DA, glucose, xanthine, catechol, choline, and cholesterol, etc.

The nanomaterials with mimetic enzyme activity can be supported on the surface of the porous and/or high specific surface area carrier to enhance and amplify the response signal, which can further improve the sensitivity of the detection of analytes. Zhan et al. [103] conceived a novel and feasible two-photon fluorescence immunosensing strategy, which can be used for carcinoembryonic antigen or glucose detection. Firstly, the yellow fluorescent carbon dots (named o-CDs) was prepared according to the OPD as the precursor through a solvothermal method. Then, the NH2-functionalized dendritic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (DMSNs)-Au NP nanostructure was synthesized by coating Au NPs on the surface of DMSNs, having GOX-like activity. Using DMSNs as a carrier can increase the number of modified Au NPs, the response signal can be enhanced to achieve highly sensitive detection of the target analyte.

The OXD-like activity of 2D nanomaterials exhibit superior catalytic performance due to the abundant active sites exposed on the surface. Cys is involved in the catalysis and post-translational modifications of proteins in living organisms. The abnormal level of Cys in human body is an important indicator of some diseases, such as drowsiness, skin lesions, liver damage, and Parkinson’s disease [104]. Song et al. [82] have prepared a 2D FeP@C nanosheets with outstanding OXD-like catalytic activity, which can oxidize non-fluorescent amplex red (AR) into high-fluorescent AR-ox in the absence of H2O2. This enzyme-like activity can be effectively inhibited by Cys, and the LOD value for the detection of Cys is as low as 0.026 μM.

Porphyrin covalent frameworks (PorCOFs) is a type of COFs with π-stacking of porphyrin units in adjacent layers, which contains a rich nitrogen group in its structure and has been widely used for analytical purpose. Functional modifications will produce more useful COFs and broaden their applications in fluorescence detection and other related fields. Zhao et al. [83] synthesized a Fe-porphyrin covalent organic framework (Fe-PorCOF) through a post-synthetic modification strategy, which exhibited POD-/OXD-like activity. It was applied for the sensitive determination of H2O2 and glucose with the LOD values of 5.3 and 4 nM, respectively.

In addition, noble metal nanoparticles and their alloys have exceptional optical and electronic properties, superior chemical stability, and excellent catalytic activity. Furthermore, the synthesized nanoflowers based on these materials can effectively increase their specific surface area, thereby possessing more active sites and better catalytic activity. Luo et al. [84] synthesized a bimetallic AgPd alloy nanoflowers that exhibites AAO mimetic activity using carbon dots as the reductant via a two-step reduction method ( Fig. 7). By utilizing the enzyme-nanozyme tandem catalytic reaction, the fluorescence of the system varies with the presence of different concentrations of ALP or organic phosphorus pesticides, which can be used for the detection of ALP in serum and organic phosphorus pesticides in vegetables and fruits. The LODs of ALP and organic phosphorus pesticides are 0.05 U/L and 0.56 μg/L, respectively.

Choline plays an important role in many physiological processes like memory, sleep, feeling, and motor activity [105]. The abnormal level of choline is ready to trigger numerous neurologic disorders, as well as dementia praecox, progressive dementedness, brain diseases, and Alzheimer's diseases [106]. Guo et al. [85] developed a facile, green, and label-free fluorescent strategy for highly sensitive and selective detection of choline based on the bifunctional MIL-101 (Fe) nanozyme with superior POD-like activity. The reaction mechanism is that the effectively generated ·OH from H2O2 under the catalysis of MIL-101 (Fe) nanozyme can act on the non-fluorescent organic ligand (terephthalic acid, TA) to generate its highly fluorescent product (2-hydroxy terephthalic acid, HTA). The developed method not only exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity towards choline with the LOD value of 20.0 nM, but also simplifies the sensing systems, reduces the detection cost, and avoids the potential risks caused by the fluorescent reagents. In Fenton-like reactions, H2O2 can be converted into highly reactive ·OH with the presence of metal catalysts, which can effectively increase the catalytic activity [107]. Razavi et al. [86] prepared ternary bismuth ferrite oxide NPs (Bi2Fe4O9 NPs) with OXD-activity for DA qualitative and quantitative detection. Fe active sites (Fe2+/Fe3+) and the d-orbitals of Fe3+ in Bi2Fe4O9 NPs operate in a Fenton-like reaction to improve the catalytic oxidization of TMB. The developed method can efficiently, rapidly, cost-effectively, and sensitively detect DA with a linear range of 0.15–50 μM and a LOD of 51 nM.

Noble metal nanoclusters (NCs) have the POD-like activity due to their exceptional structures, which have very small sizes near the Fermi-wavelength of conduction electrons (<1 nm), leading to their individual and size-dependent electronic transitions. NCs has a highly intensive fluorescence emission that has been frequently considered in the design of biosensors. Recently, the synergetic catalytic effect between some coupled and hybrid nanostructures has been reported to improve the detection sensitivity. Khataee et al. [87] prepared a new nanocomposite based on AgNCs decorated on WS2 NS to enhance its POD mimetic activity. The developed catalyzed TA-H2O2 reaction was used for the sensitive detection of glucose and H2O2. The produced HTA has an intense fluorescence emission, which is linearly increased with the increase in the H2O2 or glucose concentrations. The linear relationship for glucose detection was obtained in the range of 0.05 − 400 µM. In addition, bimetallic NCs receive more attention than monometallic NCs, mainly due to the synergy of the two metals performs higher stability and better catalytic capacity. Sun et al. [88] have successfully synthesized a gold-platinum bimetallic nanoclusters (Au-PtNCs) protected by guanosine monophosphate (GMP) (Au-PtNCs-GMP), which has POD-like activity and was used as a sensing platform for detecting glucose ( Fig. 8). Au-PtNCs-GMP not only serves as a fluorophore, but also possesses POD-like property. The superior linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F560/F418 and glucose concentration in the range of 0.01–0.4 mM was obtained. The LOD of the method is 7 μM.

To date, several types of CAT-mimicking nanozymes have been reported, such as cerium oxide NPs, iron oxide NPs, and cobalt oxide (Co3O4) NPs. Vu et al. [108] synthesized polydopamine-coated Co3O4 nanoparticles (PDA@Co3O4 NPs) through simple DA-induced self-assembly, which exhibits CAT-like activity. Compared to Co3O4 NPs, the activity of PDA@Co3O4 NPs was enhanced possibly due to the additional radical scavenging activity of the PDA shell. The obtained PDA@Co3O4 NPs can degrade H2O2 to O2 and H2O, which inhibits the formation of extremely fluorescent HTA to decrease its fluorescent signal. Therefore, the compounds that can result in the decrease in the CAT-like activity of PDA@Co3O4 NPs can be detected. Gout is a common inflammatory arthritis because of purine metabolism and lifestyle factors. Uric acid (UA) can crystallize in peripheral joints and periarticular tissues to form the monosodium urate (MSU) crystals, causing intense pain and drastic inflammatory response [109]. Selecting a multifunctional nanozyme with UA degradation as well as H2O2 scavenging activity can achieve a self-cascade reaction in joints, providing an effective strategy for the treatment of gout. Lin et al. [110] prepared a self-cascade nanozyme Pt/CeO2 that can degrade UA and eliminate H2O2 based on its uricase- and CAT-like activities, which also can achieve highly sensitive detections of UA and H2O2.

The fluorescent CDs with the advantages of optimal size, biocompatibility, and good photo- or electro-catalytic efficiency, have been applied in many fields, including bioimaging and sensing, nanomedicine, optoelectronic devices, ion detection and catalysis. Du et al. [24] have successfully synthesized a fluorescent Ce-doped CDs (CeCDs) through a one-step hydrothermal method using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a carbon source and Ce (NO3)3 as a Ce dopant. The phosphohydrolase-like activity of CeCDs can act on bis (4-nitrophenyl) phosphate (BNPP) to generate its hydrolysis products (p-nitrophenol). The highly catalytic activity of CeCDs toward BNPP hydrolysis was enhanced due to the synergistic effect of Ce (Ⅲ) ion and metal hydroxide activation.

The nanozyme can overcome the limitations and exhibit the same or even better catalytic activity of natural enzymes. With the continuous development of nanozyme, it is imperative to make further researches on its structure and mechanism. To date, nanozymes have been successfully designed to mimic several key natural enzymes (POD, CAT, SOD, OXD, and hydrolase, etc.) and have been applied in many fields such as the detection of bioactive small-molecules compounds.
(1)
Nanozymes used in the bioactive small-molecule compounds detection are mainly based on the POD- and OXD-like activity, which can act with H2O2 generates free radicals or directly reacts with substrates to produce the changes in absorbance or fluorescence intensity. The other nanozymes (CAT, SOD, and hydrolase) have limited applications in the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds, which can be mainly used in the detection of metal ion, protein, organophosphorus pesticide, and pollutants. Thus, considering the diversity of natural enzymes, designing nanozymes with novel catalytic properties, such as hydrolases and synthases, to improve the detection selectivity for bioactive small-molecule compounds of nanozymes will be the focus of future work.
(2)
The detection analytes are limited, especially the natural small-molecules compounds (bioactive compounds extracted or synthesized from nature products). Evaluating the inhibitory effect of small-molecule compounds from natural products on enzyme is significant for the related drug discovery, such as screening of α-glucosidase inhibitors from natural products to treat diabetes, and plasma kallikrein inhibitors to treat hereditary angioedema. In addition, nanozymes based on different enzyme activity, especially hydrolase, CAT, and SOD, have certain potential in detecting bioactive small-molecule compounds from natural products.
(3)
In the analysis of real samples, for example, the crude extract of natural products, the adsorption of others interfering substances to nanozymes will affect the catalytic activity, resulting in unsatisfactory detection results. Therefore, to develop novel nanozymes with high specificity, it is necessary to further investigate the effects of interferences on their enzyme-like activity.
(4)
Fluorescence spectroscopy can achieve efficient and highly sensitive detection of samples since even a small amount of a compound can exhibit fluorescence. UV spectroscopy is less sensitive and the spectra are more overlapped than in fluorescence spectroscopy, but most organic compounds can absorb UV light. Thus, the combination of fluorescence and UV spectroscopy can realize mutual correction on the same target analyte to get more reliable results.
(5)
Recently, many small molecules, including fluorescein derivatives, acidic amino acids, guanosine triphosphate, ethylene, diamine tetra acetic acid, adenine phosphate, vitamin B3, and vitamin B6, were successively reported possessing POD-like activity, which are widely used in the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds, antioxidant capacity evaluation, and enzyme-linked immunoassay. Modifying nanomaterials using these small molecules to increase their enzyme-like activity or endow them with multiple enzyme-like activity, has great potential in improving the sensitivity and selectivity of detection methods.

In this review, the recent progress from 2018 to 2023 in the colorimetric and fluorescence analysis of bioactive small-molecule compounds based on the enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials was summarized and highlighted. Nanozymes have been successfully designed to mimic several natural enzymes such as POD and OXD, using noble metals, metal oxides, metal sulfides/metal selenides, carbon materials, and MOFs. To date, most detection strategies for the bioactive small-molecule compounds are based on the POD- and OXD-like activity of nanozymes. Different methods have been reported to improve the activity of nanozymes and enhance the sensitivity of detecting bioactive small-molecule compounds, such as the cascade reaction between natural and nanozymes, as well as preparing composite nanomaterials with multienzyme activity by introducing other elements and materials to dope or post modifying. How to further tailor the enzyme-like activity (CAT, SOD, and hydrolase, etc.) of nanozymes and endow them improved specificity and sensitivity for the detection of bioactive small-molecule compounds are two important issues in the future study.
This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFC2103300).
